title: Zero-Shot vs. Few-Shot Prompting
concept: Zero-shot asks the model to perform a task directly; few-shot includes a few exemplars (input-output pairs) in the prompt to show the pattern.

exercises.prompt: What does 'prompting' mean in the context of using AI?

options.text: Giving instructions or input to an AI
Teaching a robot how to walk

exercises.explain: Prompting is the act of writing instructions or input text to guide how an AI responds.

cards.title: Show, Don't Just Tell!
cards.body: Have you ever asked an AI to write something, but it didn't write it in the format you wanted? Sometimes, telling the AI *what* to do isn't enough. We need to show it a pattern! Let's learn how to guide AI using examples.

cards.title: What's the Difference?
cards.body: Zero-shot prompting is when you ask an AI to do something without giving it any examples. Few-shot prompting is when you show the AI a few examples (input-output pairs) of what you want before asking it to do the task.

cards.title: The Recipe Analogy
cards.body: Think of zero-shot prompting like asking a friend to 'Bake a cake' without giving them a recipe. They have to guess how you want it done. Few-shot prompting is like giving them three recipe cards of cakes you love first, then saying, 'Now make this one!' They immediately see the exact style and flavor pattern you expect.

cards.title: Seeing it in Action
cards.body:
**Zero-Shot Example:**
"Translate 'Hello' to Spanish."
(Result: "Hola")

**Few-Shot Example:**
"Great -> Bueno
Sad -> Triste
Hello -> "
(Result: "Hola")

cards.title: Temporary Patterns
cards.body: Many people think few-shot prompting actually retrains or permanently updates the AI model. It doesn't! The AI only remembers the examples for that single conversation. It is just temporarily copying the pattern you showed it.

cards.title: Visual Comparison
cards.body: Let's compare how zero-shot and few-shot prompts look when you send them to an AI.

exercises.prompt: How many examples do you provide to the AI when using zero-shot prompting?
exercises.explain: Zero-shot prompting does not provide any examples. The word 'zero' represents the number of examples given to the AI.

exercises.prompt: True or False: Few-shot prompting is useful when you want the AI to output text in a very specific, custom format.
exercises.explain: Few-shot prompting is highly effective for teaching the AI a custom format because it copies the pattern shown in the examples.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the blank to describe the prompting technique that uses examples.
exercises.sentence: When you include examples of the desired output in your prompt, you are using ________ prompting.
exercises.explain: Few-shot prompting provides examples (input-output pairs) within the prompt to guide the AI's behavior and formatting.

exercises.prompt: A user prompts an AI with: "Input: sad | Output: 😢 \n Input: angry | Output: 😡 \n Input: happy | Output:". Which output did the AI generate?

options.text: Output A: "The emoji for happy is 😊. Let me know if you need more!"
Output C: "Error: Please provide more context."

exercises.explain: Output B is the only one created with few-shot prompting because it follows the clear input-output pattern ('Input: text | Output: emoji') provided by the user's examples. Output A shows a simple zero-shot conversational reply.

exercises.prompt: You want an AI to convert emojis to text in a very specific format: '🍕 -> Pizza' and '🚗 -> Car'. What is the most reliable prompting strategy to ensure the AI outputs exactly this format on its very first try?

options.text: Use Zero-Shot prompting and hope the AI guesses the correct format.
Use Few-Shot prompting by including 3 examples of the format in your prompt.
Ask the AI to write a Python script that reformats the text afterward.
Change the temperature setting of the AI model to zero.

exercises.explain: To get a specific, custom formatting like 'Input -> Output', we use few-shot prompting. By giving a few examples first, the AI learns the pattern and copies the style perfectly. Zero-shot would likely just output a normal sentence instead of the 'Emoji -> Text' format.
